title: Dense Embeddings
concept: Compact, continuous vectors where every dimension carries some signal; strong at semantic/paraphrase matching.

exercises.prompt: When is traditional keyword search most likely to fail to find relevant documents?

options.text: When searching for synonyms or paraphrases.
When looking for exact matches of a specific serial number.
When counting the frequency of a specific word in a document.

exercises.explain: Before jumping into embeddings, let's think about text search. Traditional keyword search struggles when you use different words that mean the same thing, because it only looks for exact letter matches.

cards.title: Mapping Meaning
cards.body: How can a computer know that 'king' and 'queen' are related without just looking up definitions in a dictionary? Dense embeddings turn these concepts into points in a map of meaning.

cards.title: What are Dense Embeddings?
cards.body: Dense embeddings are compact, continuous lists of numbers (vectors) where every single position contains a meaningful value. These values represent hidden semantic traits, allowing the computer to capture subtle relationships between words.

cards.title: The Flavor Profile
cards.body: Imagine a dense embedding like a rich recipe card. Instead of just listing ingredients, every single rating (sweetness, spiciness, texture) has a number filled in, giving a complete, multi-dimensional profile of the dish.

cards.title: Semantic Matching in Action
cards.body: The sentences 'I love puppies' and 'Dogs are my favorite' share zero words, but their dense embeddings look very similar numerically because they share a close semantic space.

cards.title: Size Isn't Everything
cards.body: You might think a larger vector is always better, but dense embeddings squeeze meaning into a fixed, compact size (like 768 numbers) to keep comparisons fast and efficient.

cards.title: Dense vs. Sparse Visualized
cards.body: Compare how dense representations pack active numbers into every slot, whereas sparse representations leave most slots empty (zero).

left.text: [0.25, -0.47, 0.81, 0.12] - Every slot has a meaningful signal.

right.text: [0, 0, 1.0, 0, 0, 0, 0] - Mostly zeros with rare active values.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best describes a dense embedding?

options.text: Vectors filled mostly with zeros representing exact vocabulary words.
Compact, continuous vectors where every dimension carries some signal.
Strings of text containing dictionary definitions of words.
Sparse tables that only index punctuation marks.

exercises.explain: Dense embeddings are characterized by being compact and continuous, with a non-zero numerical value in almost every dimension of the vector.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Dense embeddings are highly effective at paraphrase matching because they capture semantic meaning rather than just exact keywords.
exercises.explain: Dense embeddings represent words and phrases as points in a continuous space, meaning they can measure how close two concepts are even if they use completely different vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the core capability of dense embeddings.
exercises.sentence: Because they represent concepts in a continuous space, dense embeddings excel at semantic matching.
exercises.explain: Dense embeddings excel at semantic matching because they capture the underlying meaning and concepts, rather than relying on exact word matches.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following representations represents a dense embedding vector?
exercises.explain: Option B is typical of a dense embedding vector produced by modern neural networks, consisting of floating-point numbers where every slot contains active signal.

exercises.prompt: Why do dense embeddings excel at semantic search tasks, such as finding that 'physician' and 'doctor' are related, even when there are no overlapping words?

options.text: They represent concepts as continuous coordinates where every dimension holds data, making them great at matching paraphrases.
They only store text as exact keyword indexes with mostly empty spaces, making them fast but literal.
They completely discard semantic relationships to save memory space.
They require words to match letter-for-letter to find any similarity.

exercises.explain: Dense embeddings compress broad semantic concepts into continuous numerical arrays where every single slot contains active signal. This makes them incredibly powerful for identifying paraphrases or matching concepts that use entirely different words but share the same underlying meaning.
